## - - - META FREE FEW SHOT LEARNING VIA REPRESENTA TION LEARNING WITH WEIGHT AVERAGING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Recent studies on few-shot classification using transfer learning pose challenges to the effectiveness and efficiency of episodic meta-learning algorithms. Transfer learning approaches are a natural alternative, but they are restricted to few-shot classification. Moreover, little attention has been on the development of probabilistic models with well-calibrated uncertainty from few-shot samples, except for some Bayesian episodic learning algorithms. To tackle the aforementioned issues, we propose a new transfer learning method to obtain accurate and reliable models for few-shot regression and classification. The resulting method does not require episodic meta-learning and is called meta-free representation learning (MFRL). MFRL first finds low-rank representation generalizing well on meta-test tasks. Given the learned representation, probabilistic linear models are fine-tuned with few-shot samples to obtain models with well-calibrated uncertainty. The proposed method not only achieves the highest accuracy on a wide range of few-shot learning benchmark datasets but also correctly quantifies the prediction uncertainty. In addition, weight averaging and temperature scaling are effective in improving the accuracy and reliability of few-shot learning in existing meta-learning algorithms with a wide range of learning paradigms and model architectures.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Currently, the vast majority of few-shot learning methods are within the general paradigm of metalearning (a.k.a. learning to learn) (Bengio et al., 1991; Schmidhuber, 1987; Thrun & Pratt, 1998), which learns multiple tasks in an episodic manner to distill transferrable knowledge (Vinyals et al., 2016; Finn et al., 2017; Snell et al., 2017). Although many episodic meta-learning methods report state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, recent studies show that simple transfer learning methods with fixed embeddings (Chen et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2020) can achieve similar or better performance in few-shot learning. It is found that the effectiveness of optimization-based meta-learning algorithms is due to reusing high-quality representation, instead of rapid learning of task-specific representation (Raghu et al., 2020). The quality of the presentation is not quantitatively defined, except for some empirical case studies (Goldblum et al., 2020). Recent machine learning theories (Saunshi et al., 2021) indicate that low-rank representation leads to better sample efficiency in learning a new task. However, those theoretical studies are within the paradigm of meta-learning and do not reveal how to obtain low-rank representation for few-shot learning outside the realm of meta-learning. This motivates us to investigate ways to improve the representation for adapting to new few-shot tasks in a meta-free manner by taking the advantage of simplicity and robustness in transfer learning.

In parallel, existing transfer learning methods also have limitations. That is, the existing transfer learning methods may not find representation generalizing well to unseen few-shot tasks (Chen et al., 2019; Dhillon et al., 2020) , compared with state-of-the-art meta-learning methods (Ye et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Although some transfer learning methods utilize knowledge distillation and self-supervised training to achieve strong performance in few-shot classification, they are restricted to few-shot classification problems (Mangla et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020). To the best of our knowledge, no transfer learning method is developed to achieve similar performance to metalearning in few-shot regression. As such, it is desirable to have a transfer learning method that finds high-quality representation generalizing well to unseen classification and regression problems.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

The last limitation of the existing transfer learning methods in few-shot learning is the lack of uncertainty calibration. Uncertainty quantification is concerned with the quantification of how likely certain outcomes are. Despite a plethora of few-shot learning methods (in fact, machine learning in general) to improve the point estimation accuracy, few methods are developed to get probabilistic models with improved uncertainty calibration by integrating Bayesian learning into episodic metatraining (Grant et al., 2018; Finn et al., 2018; Yoon et al., 2018; Snell & Zemel, 2021). Few-shot learning models can be used in risk-averse applications such as medical diagnosis (Prabhu et al., 2019). The diagnosis decision is made on not only point estimation but also probabilities associated with the prediction. The risk of making wrong decisions is significant when using uncalibrated models (Begoli et al., 2019). Thus, the development of proper fine-tuning steps to achieve well-calibrated models is the key towards practical applications of transfer learning in few-shot learning.

In this paper, we develop a simple transfer learning method as our own baseline to allow easy regularization towards more generalizable representation and calibration of prediction uncertainty. The regularization in the proposed transfer learning method works for regression and classification problems so that we can handle both problems within a common architecture. The calibration procedure is easily integrated into the developed transfer learning method to obtain few-shot learning models with good uncertainty quantification. Therefore, the resulting method, called Meta-Free Representation Learning (MFRL), overcomes the aforementioned limitations in existing transfer learning methods for few-shot learning. Our empirical studies demonstrate that the relatively overlooked transfer learning method can achieve high accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty in few-shot learning when it is combined with the proper regularization and calibration. Those two tools are also portable to meta-learning methods to improve accuracy and calibration, but the improvement is less significant compared with that of transfer learning.

We use stochastic weight averaging (SWA) (Izmailov et al., 2018), which is agnostic to loss function types, as implicit regularization to improve the generalization capability of the representation. We also shed light on that the effectiveness of SWA is due to its bias towards low-rank representation. To address the issue of uncertainty quantification, we fine-tune appropriate linear layers during the meta-test phase to get models with well-calibrated uncertainty. In MFRL, hierarchical Bayesian linear models are used to properly capture the uncertainty from very limited training samples in few-shot regression, whereas the softmax output is scaled with a temperature parameter to make the few-shot classification model well-calibrated. Our method is the first one to achieve well-calibrated few-shot models by only fine-tuning probabilistic linear models in the meta-test phase, without any learning mechanisms related to the meta-training or representation learning phase.

Our contributions in this work are summarized as follows:

- We propose a transfer learning method that can handle both few-shot regression _and_ classification problems with performance exceeding SOTA.
- For the first time, we empirically find the implicit regularization of SWA towards low-rank representation, which is a useful property in transferring to few-shot tasks.
- The proposed method results in well-calibrated uncertainty in few-shot learning models while preserving SOTA accuracy.
- The implicit regularization of SWA and temperature scaling factor can be applied to existing meta-learning methods to improve their accuracy and reliability in few-shot learning.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Episodic meta-learning** approaches can be categorized into metric-based and optimization-based methods. Metric-based methods project input data to feature vectors through nonlinear embeddings and compare their similarity to make the prediction. Examples of similarity metrics include the weighted _L_ 1 metric (Koch et al., 2015), cosine similarity (Qi et al., 2018; Vinyals et al., 2016), and Euclidean distance to class-mean representation (Snell et al., 2017). Instead of relying on predefined metrics, learnable similarity metrics are introduced to improve the few-shot classification performance (Oreshkin et al., 2018; Sung et al., 2018). Recent metric-based approaches focus on developing task-adaptive embeddings to improve few-shot classification accuracy. Those task-adaptive embeddings include attention mechanisms for feature transformation (Fei et al., 2021; Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018; Ye et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021), graph neural networks (Garcia & Estrach,

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

2018), implicit class representation (Ravichandran et al., 2019), and task-dependent conditioning (Oreshkin et al., 2018; Yoon et al., 2020; 2019). Although metric-based approaches achieve strong performance in few-shot classification, they cannot be directly applied to regression problems.

Optimization-based meta-learning approaches try to find transferrable knowledge and adapt to new tasks quickly. An elegant and powerful meta-learning approach, termed model-agnostic metalearning (MAML), solves a bi-level optimization problem to find good initialization of model parameters (Finn et al., 2017). However, MAML has a variety of issues, such as sensitivity to neural network architectures, instability during training, arduous hyperparameter tuning, and high computational cost. On this basis, some follow-up methods have been developed to simplify, stabilize and improve the training process of MAML (Antoniou et al., 2018; Flennerhag et al., 2020; Lee & Choi, 2018; Nichol et al., 2018; Park & Oliva, 2019). In practice, it is very challenging to learn high-dimensional model parameters in a low-data regime. Latent embedding optimization (LEO) attempts to learn low-dimensional representation to generate high-dimensional model parameters (Rusu et al., 2019). Meanwhile, R2-D2 (Bertinetto et al., 2019) and MetaOptNet (Lee et al., 2019) reduce the dimensionality of trainable model parameters by freezing feature extraction layers during inner loop optimization. Note that the proposed method is fundamentally different from R2-D2 and MetaOptNet because our method requires neither episodic meta-learning nor bi-level optimization.

**Transfer learning** approaches first learn a feature extractor on all training data through standard supervised learning, and then fine-tune a linear predictor on top of the learned feature extractor in a new task (Chen et al., 2019). However, vanilla transfer learning methods for few-shot learning do not take extra steps to make the learned representation generalizing well to unseen meta-test tasks. Some approaches in this paradigm are developed to improve the quality of representation and boost the accuracy of few-shot classification, including cooperative ensembles (Dvornik et al., 2019), knowledge distillation (Tian et al., 2020), and auxiliary self-supervised learning (Mangla et al., 2020). Nevertheless, those transfer learning methods are restricted to few-shot classification. MFRL aims to find representation generalizing well from the perspective of low-rank representation learning, which is supported by recent theoretical studies (Saunshi et al., 2021). Furthermore, MFLR is the first transfer learning method that can handle both few-shot regression and classification problems and make predictions with well-calibrated uncertainty.

## 3 BACKGROUND

## 3.1 EPISODIC META-LEARNING

In episodic meta-learning, the meta-training data contains _T_ episodes or tasks, where the _τ[th]_ episode consists of data _Dτ_ = _{_ ( **x** _τ,j,_ **y** _τ,j_ ) _}j[N]_ =1 _[τ]_[with] _[ N][τ]_[samples. Tasks and episodes are used interchange-] ably in the rest of the paper. Episodic meta-learning algorithms aim to find common model parameters _θ_ which can be quickly adapted to task-specific parameters _φτ_ ( _τ_ = 1 _, ..., T_ ). For example, MAML-type algorithms assume _φτ_ is one or a few gradient steps away from _θ_ (Finn et al., 2017; 2018; Grant et al., 2018; Yoon et al., 2018), while other meta-learning approaches assume that _φτ_ and _θ_ share the parameters in the feature extractor and only differ in the top layer (Bertinetto et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2019; Snell et al., 2017).

## 3.2 STOCHASTIC WEIGHT AVERAGING

The idea of stochastic weight averaging (SWA) along the trajectory of SGD goes back to Polyak–Ruppert averaging (Polyak & Juditsky, 1992). Theoretically, weight averaging results in faster convergence for linear models in supervised learning and reinforcement learning(Bach & Moulines, 2013; Lakshminarayanan & Szepesvari, 2018). In deep learning, we are more interested in tail stochastic weight averaging (Jain et al., 2018), which averages the weights after _T_ training epochs. The averaged model parameters _θ_ SWA can be computed by running _s_ additional training epochs using SGD

## 4 METHODOLOGY

The proposed method is a two-step learning algorithm: meta-free representation learning followed by fine-tuning. We employ SWA to make the learned representation low-rank and better generalize to meta-test data. Given a meta-test task, a new top layer is fine-tuned with few-shot samples to obtain probabilistic models with well-calibrated uncertainty. Note that MFRL can be used for both regression and classification depending on the loss function. The pseudocode of MFRL is presented in Appendix A.1.

A Hessian matrix with a lot of small eigenvalues corresponds to a flat minimum, where the loss function is less sensitive to the perturbation of model parameters (Keskar et al., 2017). It is known that averaging the weights after SGD convergence in a local minimum basin pushes _θT_ towards the flat side of the loss valley (He et al., 2019). As a result, SWA could result in a faster decay of eigenvalues in the kernel matrix, and thus low-rank representation. Our conjecture about SWA as implicit regularization towards low-rank representation is empirically verified in Section 5.

## 4.2 FINE-TUNING

Given the learned representation, we train a new probabilistic top layer in a meta-test task using fewshot samples. The new top layer will be configured differently depending on whether the few-shot task is a regression or a classification problem.

**In a few-shot classification task** , a new logistic regression model is learned with the post-processed representation. It is difficult to select an appropriate value of _λ_ in each of the meta-test tasks due to the lack of validation data in _D_ . We instead treat _λ_ as a global hyper-parameter so that the value of _λ_ should be determined based on the accuracy on meta-validation data. Note that the selected _λ_ with high validation accuracy does not necessarily lead to well calibrated classification models. At first, we set _T_ to 1, and do grid search on the meta-validation data to find the _λ_ resulting in the highest meta-validation accuracy. However, finetuning _λ_ does not ensure good calibration. It is the temperature scaling factor that ensures the good uncertainty calibration. Similarly, we do grid search of _T_ on the meta-validation set, and choose the temperature scaling factor resulting in the lowest expected calibration error (Guo et al., 2017). Note that different values of _T_ do not affect the classification accuracy because temperature scaling is accuracy preserving.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We follow the standard setup in few-shot learning literature. The model is trained on a meta-training dataset and hyper-parameters are selected based on the performance on a meta-validation dataset. The final performance of the model is evaluated on a meta-test dataset. The proposed method is applied to few-shot regression and classification problems and compared against a wide range of alternative methods.

## 5.1 FEW-SHOT REGRESSION RESULTS

Sine waves (Finn et al., 2017) and head pose estimation (Patacchiola et al., 2020) datasets are used to evaluate the performance of MFRL in few-shot regression. We use the same backbones in literature (Patacchiola et al., 2020) to make fair comparisons. Details of the few-shot regression experiments can be found in Appendix A.2.

The results for few-shot regression are summarized in Table 1. In the sine wave few-shot regression, MFRL outperforms all meta-learning methods, demonstrating that high-quality representation can be learned in supervised learning, without episodic meta-learning. Although DKT with a spectral mixture (SM) kernel achieves high accuracy, the good performance should be attributed to the strong inductive bias to periodic functions in the SM kernel (Wilson & Adams, 2013). Additional results for MFRL with different activation functions are reported in Appendix A.3. In the head pose estimation experiment, MFRL also achieves the best accuracy. In both few-shot regression problems, SWA results in improved accuracy, suggesting that SWA can improve the quality of features and facilitate the learning of downstream tasks. In Fig. 1, uncertainty is correctly estimated by the hierarchical Bayesian linear model with learned features using just 10 training samples.

## 5.2 FEW-SHOT CLASSIFICATION RESULTS

We conduct few-shot classification experiments on four widely used few-shot image recognition benchmarks: miniImageNet (Ravi & Larochelle, 2017), tieredImageNet (Ren et al., 2018), CIFARFS (Bertinetto et al., 2019), and FC100 (Oreshkin et al., 2018). In addition, we test our approach on a cross-domain few-shot classification task from the miniImageNet to CUB. The experiment details about the few-shot classification datasets can be found in Appendix A.2. The proposed method is applied to three widely used network architectures: ResNet-12 (Lee et al., 2019; Ravichandran et al., 2019), wide ResNet (WRN-28-10) (Dhillon et al., 2020; Rusu et al., 2019), and a 4-layer convolutional neural network with 64 channels (Chen et al., 2019; Patacchiola et al., 2020) (in Appendix A.3).

The results of the proposed method and previous SOTA methods using similar backbones are reported in Table 2 and 3. The proposed method achieves the best performance in most of the experiments when compared with previous SOTA methods. Our method is closely related to Baseline++ (Chen et al., 2019) and fine-tuning on logits (Dhillon et al., 2020). Baseline++ normalizes both classification weights and features, while the proposed method only normalizes features. It allows our method to find a more accurate model in a more flexible hypothesis space, given high-quality representation. Compared with fine-tuning on logits, our method obtains better results by learning

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

a new logistic regression model on features, which store richer information about the data. Some approaches pretrain a _C_ -class classification model on all training data and then apply highly sophisticated meta-learning techniques to the pretrained model to achieve SOTA performance (Rusu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019). Our approach with SWA outperforms those pretrained-then-meta-learned models, which demonstrates that SWA obtains high-quality representation that generalizes well to unseen tasks. Compared with improving representation quality for few-shot classification via selfdistillation (Tian et al., 2020), the computational cost of SWA is significantly smaller because it does not require training models from scratch multiple times. Moreover, SWA can be applied to find good representation for both few-shot regression and classification, while previous transfer learning approaches can only handle few-shot classification problems (Mangla et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020).

MFRL is also applied to the cross-domain few-shot classification task as summarized in Table 4. MFRL outperforms other methods in this challenging task, indicating that the learned representation has strong generalization capability. We use the same hyperparameters (training epochs, learning rate, learning rate in SWA, SWA epoch, etc.) as in Table 2. The strong results indicate that MFRL is robust to hyperparameter choice. Surprisingly, meta-learning methods with adaptive embeddings do not outperform simple transfer learning methods like Baseline++ when the domain gap between base classes and novel classes is large. We notice that Tian et al. (2020) also reports similar results that transfer learning methods show superior performance on a large-scale cross-domain few-shot classification dataset. We still believe that adaptive embeddings should be helpful when the domain gap between base and novel classes is large. Nevertheless, how to properly train a model to obtain useful adaptive embeddings in novel tasks is an open question.

## 5.3 EFFECTIVE RANK OF THE REPRESENTATION

The rank of representation defines the number of independent bases. For deep learning, noise in gradients and numerical imprecision can cause the resulting matrix to be full-rank. Therefore, simply counting the number of non-zero singular values may not be an effective way to measure the rank of the representation. To compare the effective ranks, we plot the normalized singular values of the representation of meta-test data in Fig. 2, where the representation with SWA has a faster decay in singular values, thus indicating the lower effective rank of the presentation with SWA. The results empirically verify our conjecture that SWA is an implicit regularizer towards low-rank representation.

## 5.4 FEW-SHOT CLASSIFICATION RELIABILITY

The proposed method not only achieves high accuracy in few-shot classification but also makes the classification uncertainty well-calibrated. A reliability diagram can be used to check model calibration visually, which plots an identity function between prediction accuracy and confidence when the model is perfectly calibrated (DeGroot & Fienberg, 1983). Fig. 3 shows the classification reliability diagrams along with widely used metrics for uncertainty calibration, including expected calibration error (ECE) (Guo et al., 2017), maximum calibration error (MCE) (Naeini et al., 2015), and Brier score (BRI) (Brier, 1950). ECE measures the average binned difference between confidence and accuracy, while MCE measures the maximum difference. BRI is the squared error between the predicted probabilities and one-hot labels. MAML is over-confident because tuning a deep neural network on few-shot data is prone to over-fitting. Meanwhile, Proto Net and Matching Net are better calibrated than MAML because they do not fine-tune the entire network during testing. Nevertheless, they are still slightly over-confident. The results indicate that MFRL with a global temperature scaling factor can learn well-calibrated models from very limited training samples.

## 5.5 APPLICATION IN META-LEARNING

Meanwhile, we also apply SWA to episodic meta-learning methods, such Proto Net, MAML and Matching Net, to improve their classification accuracy. The results in Table 5 indicate that SWA can improve the few-shot classification accuracy in both transfer learning and episodic meta-learning. SWA is orthogonal to the learning paradigm and model architecture. Thus, SWA can be applied to a wide range of few-shot learning methods to improve accuracy.

Furthermore, the temperature scaling factor can be applied to calibrate meta-learning methods, including MAML, Proto Net, and Matching Net. The reliability diagrams in Fig. 3 indicate that the temperature scaling factor not only calibrates classification uncertainty of transfer learning approaches, such as the proposed MFRL, but also makes the classification uncertainty well-calibrated in episodic meta-learning methods. Therefore, the temperature scaling factor can be applied to a wide range of few-shot classification methods to get well-calibrated uncertainty, while preserving the classification accuracy.

## 6 DISCUSSION

SWA has been applied to supervised learning of deep neural networks (Izmailov et al., 2018; Athiwaratkun et al., 2019) and its effectiveness was attributed to convergence to a solution on the flat side of an asymmetric loss valley (He et al., 2019). However, it does not explain the effectiveness of SWA in few-shot learning because the meta-training and meta-testing losses are not comparable after the top layer is retrained by the few-shot support data in a meta-test task. The effectiveness of SWA in few-shot learning must be related to the property of the representation. Although our results empirically demonstrate that SWA results in low-rank representation, further research about their connection is needed.

Explicit regularizers can also be used to obtain simple input-output functions in deep neural networks and low-rank representation, including L1 regularization, nuclear norm, spectral norm, and Frobenius norm (Bartlett et al., 2017; Neyshabur et al., 2018; Sanyal et al., 2020). However, some of those explicit regularizers are not compatible with standard SGD training or are computationally expensive. In addition, it is difficult to choose the appropriate strength of explicit regularization. Too strong explicit regularization can bias towards simple solutions that do not fit the data. In comparison, SWA is an implicit regularizer that is completely compatible with the standard SGD training without much extra computational cost. Thus, it can be easily combined with transfer learning and meta-learning to obtain more accurate few-shot learning models. In parallel, SWA is also robust to the choice of the hyperparameters - the learning rate and training epochs in the SWA stage (see details in Appendix A.4).

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

In this article, we propose MFRL to obtain accurate and reliable few-shot learning models. SWA is an implicit regularizer towards low-rank representation, which generalizes well to unseen meta-test tasks. The proposed method can be applied to both classification and regression tasks. Extensive experiments show that our method not only outperforms other SOTA methods on various datasets but also correctly quantifies the uncertainty in prediction.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and timely contribution to the field of few-shot learning by challenging the dominance of episodic meta-learning paradigms and advocating for a simpler, more effective transfer learning approach. The core idea—Meta-Free Representation Learning (MFRL)—is conceptually clear, well-motivated, and supported by a strong narrative that links improved representation quality, generalization, and uncertainty calibration. The authors effectively position their work at the intersection of representation learning, model calibration, and few-shot learning, addressing several critical limitations in existing methods: the narrow focus on classification, poor uncertainty quantification, and the complexity of meta-training. The integration of stochastic weight averaging (SWA) as a means to induce low-rank representations is both practical and insightful, and the empirical validation across both regression and classification tasks strengthens the claim of broad applicability. However, a major concern lies in the lack of clarity around key methodological details—particularly how the "low-rank representation" is defined, measured, and causally linked to SWA in the absence of direct theoretical or ablation evidence. The claim of SWA's "implicit regularization towards low-rank" is intriguing but remains largely speculative without deeper analysis or visualization of singular values or spectral properties. Additionally, while the paper claims superiority over SOTA, a more rigorous comparison with recent meta-learning and transfer learning baselines—including computational efficiency and sensitivity to shot number—would bolster the claims. The writing is generally clear, though occasional awkward phrasing and redundancy (e.g., in motivation and contributions) could be refined. Overall, this is a strong, thought-provoking paper with high potential impact, but it would benefit significantly from deeper mechanistic analysis and more precise articulation of assumptions and limitations.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for Gaussian prior precision in regression

The current form implies $ \lambda $ is the variance, contradicting the stated definition and breaking consistency with the Gamma hyperprior on $ \lambda $, which is standard for precision. This error undermines the correctness of the Bayesian model specification in regression and is not resolvable by context. It affects inference and reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous or incorrect definition of effective rank
comments.quote: The metric $ \sum_i -\bar{\sigma}_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i $ is used to measure the effective rank of the representation, where $ \bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i/\sigma_{\text{max}} $
comments.explanation: The metric is mathematically flawed: $ \bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_{\text{max}} $ does not sum to 1, so $ \sum_i -\bar{\sigma}_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i $ is not a Shannon entropy and lacks invariance to scale. Standard effective rank uses normalized singular values $ \tilde{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i^2 / \sum_j \sigma_j^2 $ or similar. Here, the measure depends on $ \sigma_{\text{max}} $, making it ill-suited for comparing matrices of different scales. The formula may be a typographical error; the intended expression was likely $ -\sum_i \tilde{\sigma}_i \log \tilde{\sigma}_i $. As written, the metric does not reliably measure effective rank, weakening the quantitative support for the claim that SWA produces lower-rank representations.

comments.explanation: This expression is incompatible with standard effective rank definitions. Using raw singular values $ \sigma_i $ multiplied by $ \log(\sigma_i / \sigma_{\text{max}}) $ produces a scale-dependent, non-normalized quantity that does not reflect rank. It conflicts with the entropy-based formulations used in the literature. The summary defines $ \bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_{\text{max}} $, which preserves total energy and is not a probability distribution, further invalidating the form. This suggests either a severe notation error or a fundamental misunderstanding. It overlaps heavily with the issue on ambiguous effective rank definition and is merged into that consolidated item.

comments.title: Ambiguity in SWA application to episodic meta-learning methods
comments.quote: Meanwhile, we also apply SWA to episodic meta-learning methods, such Proto Net, MAML and Matching Net, to improve their classification accuracy.
comments.explanation: SWA is defined for single-model SGD trajectories, but episodic meta-learning uses bi-level optimization (inner and outer loops). The paper does not clarify how SWA is adapted: is averaging done over outer-loop iterations, inner-loop steps, or both? Is it applied to meta-parameters or per-task parameters? Without algorithmic details, the method is not reproducible. This is especially problematic as it supports comparative results in Table 1, but the technique is underspecified and potentially misleading.

comments.title: Misleading claim about temperature scaling preserving accuracy
comments.quote: Note that different values of _T_ do not affect the classification accuracy because temperature scaling is accuracy preserving.
comments.explanation: The claim assumes temperature scaling is applied post-hoc (after training), as in Guo et al. (2017). However, in MFRL, temperature $ T $ is tuned during fine-tuning on few-shot data and influences the optimization trajectory. In low-data settings, this can affect convergence and accuracy—e.g., high $ T $ may blur gradients and cause underfitting. Thus, the accuracy-preserving property does not hold when $ T $ is integrated into training. The statement misleads by overgeneralizing a post-hoc result to an end-to-end training context, risking misinterpretation of the method’s behavior.

However, the summation uses a symbol _[]_ which appears to be an OCR artifact — most likely intended as the summation symbol Σ. comments.title: Inconsistent Notation Between _θ_ and _θf_ in Representation Learning
comments.quote: Let _θ_ = [ _θf ,_ **W** ], where _θf_ represents parameters in the feature extractor and **W** denotes the parameters in the top linear layer.
comments.explanation: At first glance, writing _θ_ = [ _θf ,_ **W** ] suggests a concatenation of parameters, which is typical. However, later in Section 4.2, it says: 'After representation learning is complete, **W** is discarded and _θf_ is frozen'. This implies that the representation _h(x)_ depends only on _θf_, which is consistent. The issue is subtle: in Eq. 2 and Eq. 3 (not shown fully, but referenced), the loss is minimized over _θ_ = [_θf, W_], yet SWA is applied to _θT_, the final weights. Then _θf_ is kept and **W** discarded. This is fine procedurally. However, the notation _θ_ = [_θf, W_] might mislead a reader into thinking that SWA is applied only to _θf_, but the text says SWA is applied along the SGD trajectory, which includes both. The method is sound: train full model, apply SWA to all weights, then discard **W** and keep _θf_ for downstream use. The notation is slightly informal (using [_θf, W_] as if it's a vector concatenation), but this is conventional in deep learning. No real inconsistency. Resolved by context.

comments.title: Misstatement of Temperature Scaling's Effect on Accuracy
comments.quote: Note that different values of _T_ do not affect the classification accuracy because temperature scaling is accuracy preserving.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that temperature scaling does not affect accuracy because it's 'accuracy preserving'. But this is only true under specific conditions. Temperature scaling modifies the softmax logits: _p(y=c)_ = softmax(_f(x)_ / _T_). While it's true that the *predicted class* (the argmax) remains unchanged if _T > 0_, this holds only when the logits are scaled uniformly. In most cases (and assumed here), this is true, and accuracy (as in top-1 classification accuracy) is indeed preserved. This is a well-known fact (Guo et al., 2017). So the claim is correct for standard evaluation metrics. However, if accuracy were measured via expected calibration error (ECE) or Brier score, it would change. But in context, 'accuracy' clearly refers to top-1 error. So the statement is correct as intended. Slight ambiguity in terminology, but resolvable by community norms.

comments.title: Unjustified Claim About Hessian Approximation in Gauss-Newton Context
comments.quote: For linearized networks with squared loss, the Gauss-Newton matrix _G_ approximates the Hessian matrix _H_ when _y_ is well-described by _fθ_ ( _x_ ) (Martens, 2020).
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that the Gauss-Newton matrix _G_ approximates the Hessian _H_ when the model fits the data well. This aligns with standard second-order optimization theory (e.g., Martens, 2010; 2020): when residuals are small (i.e., _f_θ(x) ≈ y), the Hessian of the loss (for least squares) decomposes into the Gauss-Newton term and a residual-dependent term that vanishes. So the approximation improves as residuals decrease. This is correct. However, the paper uses this to argue that flatness (small Hessian eigenvalues) corresponds to good generalization, and that SWA pushes weights toward flat minima — which is also supported (e.g., Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Keskar et al., 2017). But the logical chain — that SWA → flatter minima → faster eigenvalue decay → low-rank features — is plausible, but not directly proven here. The claim about Hessian approximation itself is correct and cited. The extension to low-rank representation is a contribution the paper aims to *empirically* verify, which they do in Section 5. Therefore, the derivation up to this point is conceptually sound, though the full causal path is not established analytically. No error in this statement itself.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Hierarchical Bayesian Model in Few-Shot Regression
comments.quote: The posterior over all latent variables given the data is _p_  **w** _, λ, σ_[2] _|_ **X** _,_ **y**  ... The iterative optimization based approximate inference (Tipping, 2001) is chosen because it is highly efficient.
comments.explanation: The paper says the full posterior is _p(**w, λ, σ² | X, y)_ but then states that exact inference is intractable. It cites Tipping (2001) — likely referring to 'Sparse Bayesian Learning' or relevance vector machines — which uses type-II maximum likelihood (i.e., evidence maximization). The method optimizes _p(y | X, λ, σ²)_ w.r.t. the hyperparameters and then computes _p(**w | X, y, λ, σ²)_. This is standard. However, the transition from the full Bayesian model to this approximation is somewhat underspecified. The model defines hyperpriors as Gamma distributions but doesn't clarify whether they are used in an evidence framework or full MCMC. In few-shot settings, full hierarchical Bayes with flat hyperpriors and no data could lead to unstable estimates. The paper doesn't specify values of _a,b,c,d_ — even whether they are fixed or tuned. Later it says λ is a global hyperparameter, but here it's treated as a latent to be inferred per task. There's a contradiction: in Section 4.2, λ is both inferred *per task* (via marginal likelihood) and later called a *global* hyperparameter tuned on meta-validation data. This is a real inconsistency. Either λ is task-specific (and inferred from few data, risky) or global (fixed). The model uses a global λ (via grid search) and *separate* temperature scaling. But in the regression case, no validation data, so how is λ chosen? The paper doesn't say. This makes reproduction difficult — a key design choice is underspecified.

comments.title: Unclear or Missing Justification for SWA Inducing Low-Rank Representations
comments.quote: Our conjecture about SWA as implicit regularization towards low-rank representation is empirically verified in Section 5.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that SWA biases the solution toward low-rank representations by encouraging flat minima, which have faster decaying eigenvalues in the NTK, hence low-rank _Φ_. This is an interesting and potentially important claim. However, the logical chain relies on multiple assumptions: (1) SWA → flatter minima (supported by He et al., 2019), (2) flat minima → smaller Hessian eigenvalues, (3) H ≈ G → G has small eigenvalues, (4) G and K_C share eigenspectrum structure, (5) fast eigenvalue decay → low rank. Step (4) is plausible (Fan & Wang, 2020), but the direct link from SWA to low-rank features is not formally derived. The paper calls it a 'conjecture' and says it's empirically verified, which is acceptable. But it presents the argument in the main method section as if it's an established bridge, which might overstate the theoretical justification. A critical reader might expect a clearer demarcation between hypothesis and established fact. The claim isn't erroneous — it's properly framed as a finding — but the argumentative buildup could mislead by suggesting stronger theoretical grounding than exists. The paper later verifies it empirically, so the final claim stands, but the reasoning is a bit overstated.

comments.title: Inconsistent variable used in transfer learning method name
comments.quote: MFLR is the first transfer learning method that can handle both few-shot regression and classification problems
comments.explanation: The acronym 'MFLR' appears to be a typo for 'MFRL', which is the consistently used name throughout the paper (e.g., in the abstract, introduction, and methodology). The correct name is MFRL (Meta-Free Representation Learning), and this instance in Section 2 miswrites it as MFLR. Given that this is a single occurrence and the rest of the paper uses MFRL, it is likely an OCR or typographical error. However, as it stands uncorrected in the passage, it creates momentary confusion about whether a different method is being referenced. Since the paper summary and surrounding text clarify the correct acronym, this is not a fundamental issue, but it could mislead a careful reader scanning quickly.

comments.title: Misaligned definition of W in regression vs. classification tasks
comments.quote: For regression problems, the model learns _T_ regression tasks ( **W** = [ **w** 1 _, ...,_ **w** _T_ ] _∈_ R[(] _[p]_[+1)] _[×T]_ ) simultaneously using the loss function _LMSE_ given in Eq. 2, whereas the model learns a _C_ -class classification model[1] ( **W** = [ **w** 1 _, ...,_ **w** _C_ ] _∈_ R[(] _[p]_[+1)] _[×C]_ ) for classification problems using the loss function _LCE_ in Eq. 3.
comments.explanation: The definition of **W** for regression uses _T_ as the number of regression tasks, implying **W** ∈ ℝ^(p+1)×T, whereas for classification, **W** ∈ ℝ^(p+1)×C for C classes. This is internally consistent with the authors' setup—regression tasks are assumed to be T in number and each has a separate linear head. However, this usage risks confusion because the notation **W** is used for two different model configurations: in regression, **W** represents T task-specific output vectors, but in classification, it represents C class-specific output vectors. While the paper does clarify this implicitly via context (e.g., meta-training tasks encompassing T regression problems), the lack of explicit distinction between task indices and class indices without subscripting (e.g., **W**_τ vs **W**_c) could mislead a reader unfamiliar with multitask regression setups. The potential ambiguity arises when one might assume **W** is a universal output matrix rather than a structured collection. However, since this is standard in multitask learning (e.g., multi-output regression), and the total number of samples _N′_ sums over τ, the usage is justifiable. Thus, while slightly underspecified, the context resolves the ambiguity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in scope of SWA application to representation learning
comments.quote: We employ SWA to make the learned representation low-rank and better generalize to meta-test data.
comments.explanation: The statement appears in Section 4 but no formal description of how SWA is integrated into the representation learning phase is provided in the given passage. While SWA is described in Section 3.2 and linked to generalization, the exact mechanism—whether SWA is applied during standard training on the aggregated dataset D_tr, and how averaging affects the feature extractor h(x)—is not detailed. The paper summary notes that SWA is used for implicit regularization, but the methodology section lacks parameters or algorithmic details (e.g., how many epochs, when averaging begins, whether cyclic learning rates are used as in Izmailov et al.). This is a gap in reproducibility. However, since the paper references standard SWA practice and the pseudocode is said to be in an appendix, this may be an intentional omission from the main text. Still, without any mention of implementation choices, a reader could not reproduce the method solely from this passage. This qualifies as an underspecification for experimental reproducibility, though not fatal to understanding.

comments.title: Incorrect claim of 'first' method without full justification
comments.quote: MFRL is the first transfer learning method that can handle both few-shot regression and classification problems and make predictions with well-calibrated uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'first' is significant and appears in both the contributions and related work. While the paper correctly notes that prior transfer learning methods are limited to classification, and that uncertainty calibration is underaddressed, it does not fully substantiate that no prior method has combined both capabilities. For instance, transfer learning with Bayesian fine-tuning has been used in few-shot settings (e.g., in Gupta et al., 2020 or Finn et al., 2018’s probabilistic variants), and some methods (e.g., Tian et al., 2020) use calibration-like techniques. The claim is plausible, but without a comprehensive survey of all transfer learning + uncertainty methods for regression, it risks overstatement. However, given the paper’s empirical presentation and framing, this is likely a legitimate contribution claim within the intended scope. The context of 'probabilistic linear models fine-tuned at meta-test time without meta-training mechanisms' narrows the claim appropriately. Therefore, while the word 'first' is bold, the specific methodology (SWA + linear Bayesian fine-tuning) does appear novel as described.

comments.title: Incorrect Jacobian dimension in NTK derivation
comments.quote: where \mathbf{J} ( \mathbf{X} ) = \nabla_{\theta_f} \theta ( \mathbf{X} ) \in \mathbb{R}^{N' \times |\theta|} is the Jacobian matrix
comments.explanation:
At first glance, this expression appears problematic: the Jacobian \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{X}) is defined as 
abla_{	heta_f} 	heta(\mathbf{X}), but 	heta = [	heta_f, \mathbf{W}], so 	heta(\mathbf{X}) is not a function of parameters—it’s the parameters themselves. This suggests a misuse of notation. More critically, the dimension \mathbb{R}^{N' 	imes |	heta|} seems wrong. The standard NTK Jacobian is 
abla_	heta f_	heta(\mathbf{X}), mapping inputs through the network output, not 
abla_{	heta_f} 	heta(\mathbf{X}). Given that 	heta_f only parameterizes h(\mathbf{x}), and the full model is f_	heta(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_	au^	op [h(\mathbf{x}); 1], the correct Jacobian should be 
abla_{	heta_f} f_	heta(\mathbf{X}) \in \mathbb{R}^{N' 	imes |	heta_f|}, since \mathbf{W} is being optimized too but the derivative is only w.r.t. 	heta_f. The stated dimension \mathbb{R}^{N' 	imes |	heta|} includes |\mathbf{W}|, which shouldn't appear if the gradient is only over 	heta_f. This appears to be either an OCR error (misreading 
abla_{	heta_f} f_	heta as 
abla_{	heta_f} 	heta) or a conceptual error. Context from standard NTK definitions (Jacot et al., 2018) confirms that \mathbf{J} should be the gradient of the network output w.r.t. parameters. Thus, even assuming intended symbol correction, the current expression is incorrect. This affects the connection between representation rank and Hessian spectrum, undermining the justification for SWA’s role. While the argument qualitatively aligns with known flat-minima generalization effects, the formal derivation is flawed.

comments.title: Ambiguity in freezing and reinitializing top layer
comments.quote: After representation learning is complete, \mathbf{W} is discarded and \theta_f is frozen in a new few-shot task.
comments.explanation: The passage states that \mathbf{W} is discarded and \theta_f is frozen during fine-tuning, which aligns with transfer learning practice. However, it omits whether the new top layer (for either regression or classification) is randomly initialized or somehow adapted from the old \mathbf{W}. For reproducibility and fairness, this is critical: random vs. informed initialization affects few-shot performance. The method description assumes a fresh linear layer is trained, but it fails to specify initialization strategy (e.g., standard normal, scaled, or zero). While common practices suggest random initialization, the lack of clarity is particularly problematic given the claim of 'well-calibrated uncertainty'—posterior inference depends on prior placement, which interacts with initialization. Appendix A.1 (referenced) may resolve this, but within the main text, the method is underspecified. This is especially important in classification, where temperature scaling assumes softmax outputs; no mention is made of whether bias terms are reinitialized. Thus, a careful reader cannot fully reproduce the procedure.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of temperature symbol T
comments.quote: where T is the temperature scaling factor.
comments.explanation: The symbol T is used earlier in the paper to denote the number of meta-training tasks (e.g., in \sum_{\tau=1}^T). In the classification fine-tuning section, T is reused for the temperature scaling factor. While context helps disambiguate (subscripts vs. scalar), this overloading is error-prone and risks confusion, especially in mathematical expressions where T appears without subscript. Although common in ML to use T for temperature (following Guo et al., 2017), this introduces a notational conflict. The earlier definition in D_{tr} = \{\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Y}\} with T tasks is structural. Later, 'K-way n-shot... D = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{nK}' also introduces T as a matrix dimension. This triple use is avoidable and risks misinterpretation in derivations. A careful reader may initially infer that temperature T is linked to number of tasks, which it is not. Given that temperature is a scalar hyperparameter, a different symbol (e.g., \tau or s) would have been clearer. Context prevents serious confusion, but the reuse is suboptimal.

comments.title: Overstatement of 'first' claim in transfer learning for regression
comments.quote: MFRL is the first transfer learning method to handle both few-shot regression and classification while achieving well-calibrated uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The authors claim originality in handling regression, which may be justified—most transfer learning baselines in few-shot literature focus on classification. However, the assertion that it is the 'first' to handle both regression and calibration is questionable. Prior work like Louizos & Welling (2017) on Bayesian neural networks or probabilistic transfer learning approaches could be adapted to few-shot regression with calibrated uncertainty. While perhaps not explicitly benchmarked in the few-shot setting, the technical components (frozen features + Bayesian linear head) are not novel in isolation. More importantly, the phrase 'while achieving well-calibrated uncertainty' combines two orthogonal advances: few-shot regression and uncertainty calibration. The claim would be stronger if supported by a citation showing no prior method achieved this combination. As written, it overstates novelty. The method is indeed among the first to *explicitly* target and report on both, but 'first' implies total precedence, which may not hold. This doesn’t invalidate results but exaggerates contribution.

comments.title: Misalignment between low-rank representation claim and kernel analysis
comments.quote: We aim to find low-rank representation \mathbf{\Phi} = h(\mathbf{X}) ... equivalent to finding the conjugate kernel K_C = \mathbf{\Phi}\mathbf{\Phi}^\top with fast decaying eigenvalues.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that finding a low-rank \mathbf{\Phi} is equivalent to having fast-decaying eigenvalues in K_C = \mathbf{\Phi}\mathbf{\Phi}^\top. This is technically correct: the rank of \mathbf{\Phi} equals the number of non-zero eigenvalues of K_C. However, 'fast decay' is not equivalent to 'low-rank'—it’s a stronger condition. A matrix could have slowly decaying eigenvalues but still be low-rank (e.g., 10 equal eigenvalues). Conversely, a full-rank matrix can have fast decay. The authors likely intend to say that fast decay implies good approximation by a low-rank matrix (as in PCA), which supports better sample complexity (Saunshi et al., 2021). But as stated, 'equivalent to' is too strong. Context in the next paragraph about SWA pushing toward flat minima supports the motivation, but this specific claim overreaches. It misleads careful readers about the relationship between spectral decay and rank. This doesn’t break the argument but introduces a subtle inaccuracy in terminology that could mislead.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of few-shot regression model parameters
comments.quote: we learn a new linear regression model y = w^{\top} h(x) + \epsilon on a fixed feature extractor h(x) \in \mathbb{R}^p with few-shot training data D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n, where w denotes the model parameters and \epsilon is Gaussian noise with zero mean and variance \sigma^2.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the model appears standard: linear regression in learned features. However, h(x) \in \mathbb{R}^p implies a p-dimensional feature vector, and w \in \mathbb{R}^p would be expected. But later, in the Gaussian prior p(w | \lambda) = \prod_{i=0}^p \mathcal{N}(w_i | 0, \lambda^{-1}), the product runs from i=0 to p, indicating p+1 components (including bias). This suggests that h(x) should be [h(x); 1] — augmented with a bias term — to match the p+1 dimensions of w. The text does not explicitly state this augmentation, though it appears implicitly in the classification section (\mathbf{w}_c^\top [h(\mathbf{x}_*); 1]). The model is likely correct and consistent in practice, but the omission in the regression section could mislead a reader about the role of the bias. This is a mild ambiguity, as context (especially Eq. Key Equations and classification model) supports the implied bias augmentation.

comments.title: Unclear justification for marginal likelihood maximization in hierarchical Bayesian regression
comments.quote: The iterative optimization based approximate inference (Tipping, 2001) is chosen because it is highly efficient. The point estimation for \lambda and \sigma^2 is obtained by maximizing the marginal likelihood function p(\mathbf{y} | \mathbf{X}, \lambda, \sigma^2).
comments.explanation: The paper cites Tipping (2001) for 'iterative optimization based approximate inference' and claims it is efficient, but does not explain why this method is suitable or how it departs from exact Bayesian inference. The marginal likelihood p(\mathbf{y} | \mathbf{X}, \lambda, \sigma^2) is introduced abruptly without derivation or explanation of its form. In standard Bayesian linear regression with conjugate priors, this marginal likelihood has a known analytic form (a matrix-variate t-distribution), but the paper gives no indication of having used it. The method is critical for uncertainty calibration, so a brief explanation of how \lambda and \sigma^2 are estimated from the data alone — especially in the n \ll p regime — would strengthen the argument. While Tipping’s relevance is plausible, the paper provides minimal justification, leaving a gap in methodological transparency.

comments.title: Inconsistency between claimed generalization and table results in sine wave regression
comments.quote: In the sine wave few-shot regression, MFRL outperforms all meta-learning methods, demonstrating that high-quality representation can be learned in supervised learning, without episodic meta-learning.
comments.explanation: The supporting Table 1 shows MFRL (w.o. SWA) and MFRL achieving 0.023 and 0.016 MSE respectively, while DKT+Spectral achieves 0.08 \pm 0.06 — indeed better. However, ALPaCA is listed with 0.14 \pm 0.09, Bayesian MAML 0.54, MAML 0.67 — so MFRL does outperform those. The key issue is that DKT+Spectral uses a spectral mixture kernel tailored to periodicity, as the text rightly cautions. However, the same table includes DKT+RBF with 1.38 \pm 0.03, worse than MAML. Thus, the claim that MFRL 'outperforms all meta-learning methods' is ambiguous: it reasonably excludes DKT+Spectral due to its strong inductive bias, but the statement could mislead a reader into thinking it beats all entries in the table. The authors do qualify this, but the phrasing still overstates the conclusion slightly. A careful reader can resolve it from context, but the initial impression may be misleading.

comments.title: Missing clarification on representation learning objective for classification
comments.quote: For classification problems, the model learns a C-class classification model (\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{w}_1, ..., \mathbf{w}_C] \in \mathbb{R}^{(p+1)\times C}) for classification problems using the loss function \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} in Eq. 3.
comments.explanation: The passage mentions Eq. 3 contains \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} + \frac{\lambda}{2} \|\mathbf{W}\|^2, yet the text only refers to \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}. This creates ambiguity: was the L2 regularization applied during representation learning or only during fine-tuning? The sentence seems to imply only cross-entropy is used, but Eq. Key Equations defines a regularized version. Since the paper later introduces \lambda as a hyperparameter selected on meta-validation data in the fine-tuning stage, the use of the same \lambda during representation learning would be problematic due to circularity. Therefore, it is likely that no L2 regularization is applied during representation learning, and the authors only mean \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} in practice. But this should be clarified. As written, it risks suggesting double use of \lambda. The notation appears consistent with the summary, so this is a minor ambiguity in prose.

comments.title: Lack of specification for temperature and regularization grid search ranges
comments.quote: we set T to 1, and do grid search on the meta-validation data to find the \lambda resulting in the highest meta-validation accuracy. ... Similarly, we do grid search of T on the meta-validation set, and choose the temperature scaling factor resulting in the lowest expected calibration error.
comments.explanation: The paper describes a two-stage hyperparameter selection: first optimize \lambda for accuracy with T=1, then optimize T for calibration. This is reasonable. However, the search space for \lambda and T is not specified. In few-shot settings with limited data, results can be sensitive to the scale and resolution of the grid. For reproducibility, common practice is to report ranges (e.g., \lambda \in [10^{-4}, 10^2]) and step sizes. The method is underspecified as-is. While the likely implementation follows standard choices, the absence of detail makes independent reproduction difficult, especially for a core component affecting both accuracy and calibration. This is a real gap in methodological clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for Gaussian prior precision in regression
comments.quote: a Gaussian prior $ p(\mathbf{w} \mid \lambda) = \prod_{i=0}^{p} \mathcal{N}(w_i \mid 0, \lambda) $ is placed over $ \mathbf{w} $, where $ \lambda $ is the precision in the Gaussian prior.
comments.explanation: Initially, this appears problematic because the product of normals $ \prod_{i=0}^{p} \mathcal{N}(w_i \mid 0, \lambda) $ suggests that $ \lambda $ is the variance, not the precision. In standard Bayesian linear regression, if $ \lambda $ is precision (i.e., inverse variance), then the Gaussian should be written as $ \mathcal{N}(w_i \mid 0, \lambda^{-1}) $. The notation here seems to contradict this convention. However, the paper defines $ \lambda $ as 'precision' right after, which creates a direct conflict. This is not a case of OCR error—the symbol $ \lambda $ is used consistently throughout, and the prior is later paired with a Gamma hyperprior $ p(\lambda) = \text{Gamma}(\lambda \mid a,b) $, which is standard for precision (not variance). Thus, the error must be in the expression itself: it should state $ \mathcal{N}(w_i \mid 0, \lambda^{-1}) $ or $ \mathcal{N}(w_i \mid 0, 1/\lambda) $. The current form incorrectly implies that larger $ \lambda $ means wider variance, which contradicts both the definition and the Bayesian inference setup. This error persists even after contextual checks and undermines the correctness of the model specification.

comments.title: Misleading description of temperature scaling preserving accuracy
comments.quote: Note that different values of $ T $ do not affect the classification accuracy because temperature scaling is accuracy preserving.
comments.explanation: This statement is generally accepted in the literature: temperature scaling preserves the argmax of the softmax output, so top-1 accuracy is unaffected in expectation when the model is already well-separated. However, in practice—especially in few-shot settings with very small test sets—rescaling logits can change the decision boundary due to finite precision or tied scores. The paper presents this as an unconditional truth. While the authors are correct in the idealized case, stating it as a blanket rule without qualification (e.g., 'preserves accuracy under unambiguous predictions') could mislead readers into assuming no accuracy fluctuation occurs in experiments. The claim is reasonable in intent, but overgeneralized. Given that the paper emphasizes calibration and reliability, omitting this caveat is a moderate issue. However, because it aligns with common usage (e.g., Guo et al., 2017), and the practical impact is minor, the severity is reduced.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'effective rank' metric without formal definition
comments.quote: The metric $ \sum_i -\bar{\sigma}_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i $ is used to measure the effective rank of the representation, where $ \bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i/\sigma_{\text{max}} $.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears to be referencing the Shannon entropy of normalized singular values, often used as a continuous measure of 'effective rank.' However, $ \bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_{\text{max}} $ are not normalized to sum to 1, so $ \sum_i -\bar{\sigma}_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i $ is not a valid entropy. The correct form would use $ \tilde{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sum_j \sigma_j $ or $ \sigma_i^2 / \sum_j \sigma_j^2 $ (the latter being common in 'effective rank' definitions, e.g., in Roy & Vetterli, 2007). Using $ \bar{\sigma}_i $ (scaled by max, not sum) distorts the measure and makes it non-monotonic with respect to rank. While Figure 2 shows faster decay with SWA, the metric itself is not a standard or well-defined proxy for effective rank, and the text does not justify its use. The figure labels include values like 'log i = 68.49', which seem unrelated to the sum expression. This suggests a potential OCR or notation confusion—perhaps the authors meant to write the entropy of $ \sigma_i^2 / \|\sigma\|_1 $, but wrote $ \bar{\sigma}_i $ instead. Given the surrounding evidence (faster decay = lower effective rank), the conclusion may still be valid, but the formal justification is underspecified and potentially incorrect.

comments.title: Incorrect Jacobian definition in NTK section
comments.quote: where $ \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{X}) = \nabla_\theta f_\theta(\mathbf{X}) \in \mathbb{R}^{N' \times |\theta|} $ is the Jacobian matrix
comments.explanation:
This conflicts with standard NTK definitions. In the NTK framework, $ \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{X}) $ should be the Jacobian of the network outputs with respect to the parameters, evaluated at a given input $ \mathbf{X} $. However, $ f_	heta(\mathbf{X}) $ is typically a vector in $ \mathbb{R}^{N'} $ (outputs for each sample), so $ 
abla_	heta f_	heta(\mathbf{X}) $ would indeed be $ N' 	imes |	heta| $, which matches. But in classification, outputs are often $ \mathbb{R}^{N' 	imes C} $, so the full Jacobian would need to be vectorized. The paper assumes regression-like settings for linearization, so the dimensionality may be acceptable. More critically, earlier in the summary $ \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{X}) = 
abla_	heta f_	heta(\mathbf{X}) $ is used, but in the text it says $ 
abla_{	heta_f} f_	heta(\mathbf{X}) $? No—the passage uses $ 
abla_	heta $, consistent with the summary. However, in Section 4.1, it says 'linearize the neural network by the first-order Taylor expansion at $ 	heta_T $' and defines $ f_	heta(\mathbf{X}) $, but does not clarify whether $ f_	heta $ includes the entire network or just the last layer. Given that $ 	heta = [	heta_f, \mathbf{W}] $, and in fine-tuning $ \mathbf{W} $ is discarded, it's likely $ f_	heta $ is the full output. Thus, the Jacobian includes gradients w.r.t. all parameters. This is correct for NTK. No actual error—initial concern was resolved by context.

comments.title: Missing clarification on hyperparameter selection for $ \lambda $ in classification
comments.quote: We instead treat $ \lambda $ as a global hyper-parameter so that the value of $ \lambda $ should be determined based on the accuracy on meta-validation data.
comments.explanation: The paper states that $ \lambda $ is selected for accuracy on meta-validation data, and $ T $ for calibration (via ECE). However, it does not clarify whether $ \lambda $ is tuned *once* for all tasks or per-task using meta-validation meta-tasks. Given that $ \lambda $ is called a 'global hyper-parameter', it's likely shared across tasks, but the lack of clarity here affects reproducibility. How many values were tried? Was there a nested loop? Since this is critical for fair comparison (e.g., vs. methods that tune per task), the underspecification is a moderate concern. The method is still implementable with assumptions, but key design choices are left implicit.

comments.title: Ambiguity in SWA application to meta-learning methods
comments.quote: Meanwhile, we also apply SWA to episodic meta-learning methods, such Proto Net, MAML and Matching Net, to improve their classification accuracy.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears problematic because SWA (Stochastic Weight Averaging) is typically applied during the training of a single model by averaging weights along an SGD trajectory. However, episodic meta-learning methods like MAML involve a bi-level optimization process with per-task adaptation, so it is unclear how the SWA procedure is adapted to the meta-training loop. The paper does not specify whether SWA is applied to the global parameters after each outer-loop update (e.g., averaging meta-parameters over outer-loop iterations), nor does it clarify whether SWA is used during inner-loop adaptation or only in the meta-training phase. While SWA has been used in meta-learning contexts before (e.g., SWA-MAML), the description here is too vague to reproduce. The omission is especially concerning because the results in Table  ContentValues

comments.title: Inconsistency in effective rank definition between text and summary
comments.quote: $\overline{\text{rank}} = \sum_i \sigma_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i$
comments.explanation: I was initially concerned that the expression $\sum_i \sigma_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i$ does not resemble any standard form of effective rank. Typically, effective rank is defined via entropy of normalized singular values, e.g., $ \exp\left(-\sum_i \bar{\sigma}_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i\right) $ or $ -\sum_i \bar{\sigma}_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i $, where $\bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sum_j \sigma_j$. The given formula uses $\sigma_i$ (not normalized by sum) multiplied by $\log \bar{\sigma}_i$, where $\bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_\text{max}$ — this is inconsistent with standard definitions. Moreover, $\sigma_i$ are raw singular values, so the term $\sigma_i \log(\sigma_i / \sigma_\text{max})$ is not dimensionless and scales with $\sigma_\text{max}$, making it non-invariant to scale. Given that the authors claim this measure reflects lower effective rank with faster decay, they likely meant $\sum_i \bar{\sigma}_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i$, an entropy-based measure. Alternatively, if $\bar{\sigma}_i$ is intended to be probability-normalized ($\sum \bar{\sigma}_i = 1$), the notation is misleading. The summary says $\bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_\text{max}$, which preserves the sum and is not a probability distribution. This suggests the formula may be incorrectly stated. Given that in Section 5.3 (not shown here) they likely present empirical singular value decay, and the claim about SWA accelerating decay is plausible, the intended meaning is probably the entropy of the normalized singular spectrum. But as written, the formula is mathematically questionable and inconsistent with standard usage.

comments.title: Misleading claim about temperature scaling preserving accuracy
comments.quote: Note that different values of _T_ do not affect the classification accuracy because temperature scaling is accuracy preserving.
comments.explanation: This statement is generally true in *post-hoc* calibration settings where temperature scaling is applied *after* training, and only the softmax temperature is tuned on a validation set without retraining. However, in the context of MFRL, temperature scaling is applied during fine-tuning on few-shot support data — the logistic regression layer is trained with a cross-entropy loss that includes temperature scaling as part of the model output. The paper treats _T_ as a global hyperparameter selected on meta-validation data, which suggests it is tuned once and used across tasks. But if _T_ is part of the model during fine-tuning, changing _T_ *does* affect the optimization path and final classifier, potentially impacting accuracy — particularly in low-data regimes where over-regularization via high temperature could underfit. The claim assumes temperature scaling is purely a monotonic transformation of logits that doesn’t change the decision boundary, which holds only if applied *after* training. Since the fine-tuning procedure involves training with the scaled softmax, the accuracy-preserving property may not hold. The authors correctly cite Guo et al. (2017), but in that work, temperature scaling is applied post-hoc. Here, it is integrated into the training loop. This overstatement risks misleading readers about the neutrality of temperature scaling in end-to-end fine-tuning.

comments.title: Underspecification of hierarchical Bayesian linear model parameterization
comments.quote: we learn a new linear regression model _y_ = **w** _[⊤] h_ ( **x** ) + _ϵ_
comments.explanation: The expression '_y_ = **w** _[⊤] h( **x** ) + _ϵ_' appears to have an OCR error — likely '_y_ = **w** _[⊤] h_ ****( **x** ) + _ϵ_' is intended, i.e., $ y = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) + \epsilon $. This interpretation is consistent elsewhere. However, the input to the linear model is underspecified: is a bias term included? The prior is placed over all elements of **w**, including a possible intercept. But in Eq. 4 (not fully shown), if the predictive distribution depends on $ \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) $ without augmentation, then **w** must include a bias. The prior $ p(\mathbf{w} | \lambda) = \prod_{i=0}^{p} \mathcal{N}(w_i | 0, \lambda) $ suggests $ p+1 $ weights — consistent with augmenting $ \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) $ with a 1 for bias. However, this is not explicitly stated. In few-shot regression with $ n \ll p $, omitting the bias or mishandling it can break the model. While common practice includes a bias, the method description should clarify whether the representation is augmented or the prior includes a separate bias term. As written, a reader might not know how to implement the model correctly.

comments.title: Unclear connection between SWA and low-rank representation in theory
comments.quote: Our conjecture about SWA as implicit regularization towards low-rank representation is empirically verified in Section 5.
comments.explanation: The argument in Section 4.1 attempts to link SWA to low-rank representation via flat Hessian flatness and kernel eigenvalue decay. The logic chain is: SWA → flatter minima → smaller Hessian eigenvalues → faster decay of kernel eigenvalues → low-rank representation. However, this relies on several assumptions not justified in the paper. First, the equivalence between Gauss-Newton and Hessian holds only near a minimum and when the model fits the data well — but for classification, cross-entropy with softmax outputs means residuals are non-zero even at convergence. Second, flatness in parameter space does not necessarily imply low-rank in feature space: a flat minimum could still produce high-rank features. Third, the connection between NTK eigenvalues and conjugate kernel (representation) rank is theoretical and holds only in infinite-width or linearized regimes. The authors cite Fan & Wang (2020), but do not establish that their finite-width network behaves like its linearization. Thus, while the empirical results in Section 5.3 (singular value decay) may support the claim, the theoretical justification is underspecified and overreaches. The paper presents this as a conjecture, so the flaw is in presentation rather than substance — but the leap from Hessian flatness to feature rank is not trivial and should be more cautiously framed.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and timely contribution to the few-shot learning literature by challenging the prevailing meta-learning paradigm. The writing is clear, and the motivation is well-articulated, effectively positioning the work against recent findings that question the necessity of complex episodic training. The proposed Meta-Free Representation Learning (MFRL) framework is elegant in its synthesis, combining Stochastic Weight Averaging (SWA) to encourage low-rank representations with simple probabilistic fine-tuning to achieve well-calibrated uncertainty. However, the paper's most significant issue is the sheer magnitude of its claims. Stating that the method "achieves the highest accuracy on a wide range of few-shot learning benchmark datasets" for both classification and regression, while also being the first to achieve proper calibration, sets an exceptionally high bar for empirical validation. The strength of this submission will therefore hinge almost entirely on the experiments section, which must provide overwhelming and meticulously detailed evidence to support these state-of-the-art claims, including direct analysis of the low-rank property of SWA-derived representations and thorough ablations on the contribution of each component.

comments.title: Multiple OCR errors in text and equations
comments.quote:
## - - - META FREE FEW SHOT LEARNING VIA REPRESENTA TION LEARNING WITH WEIGHT AVERAGING
...The quality of the presentation is not quantitatively defined...
...where N' =[] T τ=1 N[τ]...
comments.explanation: Several OCR errors appear throughout the document. In the title, 'REPRESENTA TION' contains an extra space. In the Introduction, 'presentation' is used where 'representation' is clearly intended based on context. In the equations, the summation symbol is rendered as '[]' in the definition of N'. These are formatting errors that should be corrected for clarity.

comments.title: Strong SOTA claims without immediate evidence
comments.quote: The proposed method not only achieves the highest accuracy on a wide range of few-shot learning benchmark datasets but also correctly quantifies the prediction uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims achieving 'highest accuracy' across 'wide range' of benchmarks, which is a very strong claim. While such claims are common in abstracts and would be justified in the experiments section, readers should be aware that this requires substantial empirical validation across multiple datasets and settings. The claim about 'correctly quantifies' uncertainty is also strong, as 'correctly' implies ground truth uncertainty, which is typically unobservable.

comments.title: Typo in method name
comments.quote: Furthermore, MFLR is the first transfer learning method that can handle both few-shot regression and classification problems and make predictions with well-calibrated uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The authors consistently refer to their method as 'MFRL' (Meta-Free Representation Learning) throughout the paper, including in the abstract and title. However, in this sentence, it appears as 'MFLR'. Given the consistent usage elsewhere, this is clearly a typographical error rather than an intentional variation.

comments.title: Missing hyperparameter specifications
comments.quote: The paper mentions using SWA to improve representation quality, but doesn't specify the SWA hyperparameters... doesn't specify the actual values used for hyperparameters a, b, c, and d in the experiments.
comments.explanation: Reproducibility is affected by missing specifications for key hyperparameters.
